item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ooh the sawdust people of the town reminds me of the clear water town. I forget which SCP that one is though

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that as non-commercial licenses are not compliant with CC BY-SA 3.0, the image is not compliant and has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So the concept already had me wondering about the dwindling population, and then it says that the town "resets" after the dissipation of the outburst event. Does this mean the population also resets? Does the town refill to its original number of residents after each outburst? Or is it just the memories and mindset that resets?

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting the source of the image for staff reference : https://ccsearch.creativecommons.org/photos/8ca8cdaa-a9e8-4fd7-8638-73616fcd8f99, with some minor edits

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image used in this article was taken by AgentAX and is cc-compliant.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

On ihps author page, he defines SCP-1366 as another instance of a Nexus, a project which he was involved in the creation of. As such, crosslinking this to the Nexus Series Hub is appropriate.

Editing this:

Site-403: Site-403 is the town of Town of Siskin, ██████, USA, located in the northwestern region of █████ County. The 2010 census conducted by the Foundation shows that Site-403 has a civilian population of roughly 6000, with 400 extended families. The majority of the populace are farm or lumber workers, or work at local businesses. The total area of the town encompasses approximately 9.5km2, and is secluded in a heavily wooded area.

To this:

Site-403: Site-403 is the town of Town of Siskin, ██████, USA, located in the northwestern region of █████ County. The 2010 census conducted by the Foundation shows that Site-403 has a civilian population of roughly 6000, with 400 extended families. The majority of the populace are farm or lumber workers, or work at local businesses. The total area of the town encompasses approximately 9.5km2, and is secluded in a heavily wooded area. Standard protocol for an Asphodel Class Nexus are in place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The presence of the strikethrough points toward it being intentional IMO, possibly implying that the foundation recognized that, just because it always has, does not mean it always will.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uhhh…. Yeah. Can someone get ahold of Ihp and ask him (Ihp is a dude, right?) whether this was intentional? It's really throwing me off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1'd, hard.

Was really digging the sensationalism angle, and then the whole evacuation incident just kicked it up a further notch.

Two things, Phase 4 and 5 do indeed seem to be mixed up, like DreamwalkerFae mentions. I'd go ahead and fix that, but I'm not sure that counts as a minor grammar error.

Second, in the description, there's this:

"The writing will present itself in the form of a sensationalist headline of dubious credibility, which will may later be published as a news story in the local paper, the Siskin Gazette."

The strikethrough implies that the message carved on the log used to but no longer always shows up in the newspaper. However, this is never addressed elsewhere in the article. All examples given show that each message has a corresponding news story. Should probably get rid of the strikethrough/correction altogether and just make it:

""The writing will present itself in the form of a sensationalist headline of dubious credibility, which will later be published as a news story in the local paper, the Siskin Gazette."

without the bolding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Phases 4 and 5 seem to be mixed up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the evacuation bit. I wonder if the next containment would just be watching what happens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There were two typos in the event log in which "Outburst" was spelled without the R. I feex, because there's no way that was deliberate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Strong upvote. I loved the final piece with the "evacuation details." I can just see entire Foundation teams just throwing up their hands and saying, "Okay, FUCK this. Nevermind."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A strong upvote from me, with the evacuation details being the full sale of the article. The only minor quibble I have is the lack of dates, even redacted ones, for the events.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While it's possible that there might be an argument to make for contributors-only voting, this isn't it. Most chefs make poor, and I mean god-awful, restaurant critics. The best artists are emotionally invested in what they produce, while the best critics tend to be both agnostic and objective.

Also, putting out two paragraphs and then ending with "We shouldn't talk about this here" is not cricket.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, to be openly honest, the current wiki site already has significantly high standards. To continue the metaphor, this site is more of a fancy sit-down restaurant than a casual fast-food joint (granted, I don't eat out much, so a more experienced restaurant-goer can call me out on that). If there are certain standards required for a dish, a chef is more likely to give a constructive opinion because they're aware of the process and the requirements involved in achieving that particular dish, and they can give more reliable advice regarding any improvements that may be necessary.

That said though, people enjoying an SCP article is one thing, the article being well-written is another. Seeing as this conversation is sort of going off the topic of the SCP the discussion page belongs to, I recommend that people wanting to further the discussion do so through PM. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel that this is a poor direction to take the site in. This is basically saying that some opinions are intrinsically less valuable than others. One doesn't have to be a chef to judge if a meal is well made. The same applies to writing and skips. If this is truly how the new site will operate then I'm not sure I will want to be a part of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Minor nitpick - the outburst events all seem to be listed in order of event designation with the exception of event 2, which is between events 17 and 36 in the event log. As it's the only event to have a one-digit designation number (the others being 01, 03, etc.) is it possible that this was a mistake and that it was intended to be event 1366-Outbreak-22? I'd edit it myself but I'd just like to make sure I'm not altering something that was a deliberate choice by the author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this, and the Mad Gasser is what sold it for me. There was a real life urban legend scare about a so called mad gasser, and you took that and made it somewhat amusing. 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. No matter what the Foundation do, these people cannot be saved and that, to me, is the draw of this SCP. The town isn't secured, the anomaly isn't contained and the "people" aren't protected. Eventually, regardless of what the Foundation does, the people die.

Obviously it's not a "good" thing, but it's an interesting scenario that there are 6000 people, potentially more if outsiders are capable of moving into the town at will, who are doomed, permanently. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While reading the evacuation log, I had my hands over my mouth. Upvoted hard for that.

This is just the most bizarre collection of things, yet they all work together. The one thing that doesn't work is the terminology. 1366-Outburst event? The various names for the stages? Too flashy. It wasn't enough to keep me from upvoting, but I do think it needs rethinking.

item.title: Nope.
item.content:encoded: I'm done. Not making any more revisions. I worked hard on this, and if you don't like it? Tough, because I do.

item.title: So...
item.content:encoded:
The new evacuation thing is really not very good. It's not quite as bad as it would be for me to downvote, but it doesn't help.

Here's what I would do: What if the SCP needs a minimum number of people to work? If it has more, great, but less? It widens its area of effect. Which is a problem, because there is presumably a much bigger town near enough to it that if the area of effect were to widen (again), very bad things would happen.

The Foundation would then try to keep the population as stable as possible. Not too many innocent people coming into harm's way, but not too few that the next town over suddenly has anomalous serial killers.

I'm honestly not sure how to make evacuation work well, but this is not the way to do it. I'd be willing to talk with you a bit more about this if you PM me here or on chat.

item.title: Re: Rewrite derp.
item.content:encoded: Liked this before, LOVE it now. Very cool little piece of scifi.

item.title: Rewrite derp.
item.content:encoded:
Down with the plotholes, the awkward language, and all of the mediocrity!
Up with something that is hopefully semi-decent!

Thank you, Anaxagoras! I almost feel like giving you half-writing credit on this. (I still cannot believe that is an actual word.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…non-contributor upvotes won't by themselves help an article avoid deletion on the new site…

=o(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is mediocre, and while it's not badly written by any means, it's still mediocre enough for me to downvote.

I like Anax's idea, on one hand, but on the other hand, +23 isn't bad. One the other other hand, how many of your upvotes are from contributors? May be something to keep in mind considering that non-contributor upvotes won't by themselves help an article avoid deletion on the new site. I know that's a little while away, but… yeah. (I don't actually know how many non-contrib votes you have though - just saying keep it in mind!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This. You're at +24, that's very far from awful.

-edit-

That's not to say you shouldn't revise it, just that I wouldn't delete it to do so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The changes boost the article a lot. Plus, they'll hopefully be coupled with significantly cleaned up formatting and designations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article is doing pretty well. You may just want to make some small modifications, in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, I didn't see you asking for feedback. Haven't been on chat much lately.

If you'd like to incorporate that angle, I'd be glad to discuss it with you further if you like. I'd personally probably repost, but it's honestly just a matter of personal preference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this. It's basically a rumor mill, or an urban legend machine. The saw does it for me. If it was just a printing press, meh. Not too attention grabbing. But a sawmill? That's just bizarre. Upvote get.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…

This is exactly why people ask for feedback before they post SCPs.

I want to write in this angle, but…

Gahhhh. Would it be bad to tear this down and revise it? Or should I just redo a draft here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, yeah. We can give an already magical sawmill some additional capabilities, because it's already established to be not conforming to the laws of reality. But the notion that a town of several thousand people acting contrary to how we know people work? We know how people work, and it's one of the areas we're least willing to stretch suspension of disbelief.

That's why bad tone in interviews is so grating. Strictly speaking, is it less likely that a researcher peppers his dialog with rather implausibly alliterative curses than that there is a crystal that conjures ever larger worms in an attempt to devour the moon? Probably not. But it's really only the first of these that is guaranteed to get downvoted into oblivion.

I really do think the fake people is a good way to spin it. What do you think, author?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's funny to me, this "Magic sawmill makes thousands of ersatz humans in order to manifest thought experiment" idea would be more plausible than "Town full of people has no security leaks".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the addition of C-40, the mad cow disease. Fills the evacuation plothole pretty good. If they try, the SCP takes countermeasures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, it just seems odd to me that the town can really be quarantined like that. Seriously, where do they get food from? What if family wants to visit? There's no way you can seal it off like that without people noticing that it's being sealed off. Doktori is right, people would mention the anomalous stuff during the events.

I have an idea that would fix all the plot holes: The people in the town aren't really people. They're constructs spawned by the Sawmill. They think they're human, and they're genetically human, but they're really just playthings created by the malevolence lurking in the Sawmill. They don't have families to visit them, they don't use Twitter to talk outside. They don't even need food delivered. Unbeknownst to everyone in the town (because they would forget instantly if they saw), the Sawmill provides.

It doesn't get vindictive if an evacuation is attempted. The Foundation's tried it several times. When people get too far from the town, they just crumble into sawdust, and new ones appear in the community to take their place. Ultimately, the murders/suicides/whatever caused by the Sawmill are temporary as well. Though it's tricky to speculate at the motives of the Sawmill, it almost seems as if it's searching for the perfect reaction. A moral panic to end all moral panics, and, indeed, all morals.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The dissipation effect means that the people won't remember what happened, but what about during the event? You would think someone would make a call to family outside the area, or a Facebook post, or a tweet. Consider not having electronics work during an episode, or at least mention that analysis of outgoing calls, etc, do not say anything about the unusual events occurring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Residents of Site-403 discuss SCP-1366-B-2 in public and among their families.

Do they not share it with friends and family outside the town? What with email and instant messaging and Twitter, it seems like it would be easy for the news to spread outside of the town.

All SCP-1366-B-2 will be treated as completely factual by the populace, however.

Are visitors to the town affected? If so, how do Foundation agents in the town avoid being affected? if visitors aren't affected, do they simply brush it off, or does the anomly prevent them from becoming aware of the sensationalist news reports?

Sources of the article are never cited, and fictional cities in several states and countries are often named.

What if an unaffected person tries to point out that those cities don't exist?

At this point, the majority of the populace believes that the Source is of little concern; about 5% of the population show mild interest or concern.

What do you mean by "the Source"? The subject of the sensationalist reports? The lumbermill?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the ridiculous sensationalism angle, honestly— and that it centers around a sawmill makes it all creepy and disjunct. I thought you were cognizantly over-the-top. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not having seen the original version of this let me just say that in its current form this is pretty spectacular. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm, I… just don't like it all that much. It's like if you took The Village and replaced the monsters with a magical log Whut Makes You Go Crazy. No downvote, though, because I don't not like it that much.

It's oak-y, I guess. /shot

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I maintain my objection, but you're making it tough not to like this. Downvote removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I tried fixing the language….

But I think I made it worse. D:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Allow me to address some of the criticism I'm getting:

too… intentionally evil and over-the-top.

Regarding the over the top part, I can see where you're coming from. However, the article does say:

the writing will present itself in the form of a sensationalist headline of dubious credibility

And what is Sensationalism if not over the top? Even so, it could deserve to be toned down a little.

I'm also unsure why the Foundation doesn't just evacuate the town.

I KNEW there was a plot hole I forgot to plug up. Dammit. I'll get around to fixing that in post.

It's/its mixup, downvoted instantly

Its/It's been fixed. :3

I see no way that the Foundation would be cluing all the authority figures in the town in to the truth and no good reason for them to want to in the first place.

Gaaah so many plot holes what the fuck Ihpkmn what the fuuuuck.

This one has potential, but you need to clean up some of the language.

I agree with that, and am editing the language at the same time as I'm typing this.

Needs tags, though

That was just a screw-up on my part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked it, but the escaped civilian sorta broke the flow for me. No vote for now. I think it would be better if the SCP just ended at the chart without the "prisoners escape" article.

There's also some minor formatting errors (missing hyphens, extra zeroes, etc).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's possible that such an arrangement could be written up in a credible fashion, lots of articles refer to cover stories. It's not, though. As written, the towns administration is aware of what's going on and nobody either leaks the information or simply leaves, which isn't (for me, at least) believable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really nice - sort of a cognitohazard Daily Mail. Needs tags, though
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad. I would personally modify the last log to not mention the "unknown people" sealing the doors and windows, since the unknown people seem like some kind of a writer's tool to get more suspense - but there is no foreshadowing to these people, so they don't seem very plausible when they are mentioned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a lot easier to run things if the authority figures around town aren't actively fighting you, which they would be if they have no idea what's going on. The Foundation probably isn't briefing them on the existence of the Foundation itself or anything, they're probably just claiming to be government agents, and, due to prior agreements, the US government will back up their story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one has potential, but you need to clean up some of the language. You have 1366-A, 1366-B-1 and B-2, 1366-C, and 1366-y, not to mention Site 403. Also, 1366-C and 1366-B-2 are both the newspaper that has evil whatsits on the front page, but I think B-2 is the individual edition of the newspaper and -C is the company that makes newspapers. Then you have the designation of the text contained in B-1 as The Source, which is counterintuitive because it's not the source of anything, but the message: the sawmill, the log, or whatever is behind the sawmill is The Source, not the text, especially when it's referring to the idea, not the original text.

Also, there's apparently nothing bad that happens if the story doesn't get out, so why isn't the containment procedure "Don't let Siskin have a newpaper. Buy them out and smash the presses."

Holding my vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do dig the idea of a moral-panic-based anomaly, but this asks too much suspending of the disbelief. I see no way that the Foundation would be cluing all the authority figures in the town in to the truth and no good reason for them to want to in the first place. Also there is the question, as mentioned above, of why they don't just evacuate (or terminate, if worst comes to worst) the residents?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As far as evacuating the town, perhaps the mill could produce a XK-class prediction if the an evacuation is attempted?
Maybe some kind of ritual if performed would keep the worst of the Mill's clairvoyance at bay?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is bloody fantastic. It feels like a book Stephen King never got around to writing (maybe like a sequel to Tommyknockers)

Emphatically upvoted!
+1

Edit

zoosadism!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ^this. Unless the author's not active, they can fix it themselves. Otherwise, it just teaches them that they can screw up as much as they want, grammar-wise, and somebody will fix it for them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But then they won't learn from their mistake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jekeled, you know you're allowed to go in and fix minor grammatical errors like that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's/its mixup, downvoted instantly

Apart from that, I like this, but I agree, the quarantine thing that I suggested does seem too over the top. I shouldn't have suggested containment procedures based on a one-line summary of an SCP, and I apologize for leading you down the wrong path, containment-procedure wise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm also unsure why the Foundation doesn't just evacuate the town. There's no real indications of a lasting effect, so it seems that, perhaps with some preliminary experimentation, it should be safe to do. Additionally, it's unclear how the town is able to continue functioning under quarantine of the Foundation. Also, a carved log is just weird.
